beats.text: Here's matrix A — two eigenvectors load into the columns of matrix S, side by side.
The factorization appears: A equals S times Lambda times S-inverse. Lambda sits in the middle — a diagonal matrix with eigenvalues lambda-one and lambda-two on the diagonal, zeros everywhere else.
Think of S as a lens. It rotates the grid to align with the eigenvectors. In those new coordinates, A acts exactly like Lambda — pure stretching along each axis, no mixing.

Now raise A to the power k. The formula collapses to S times Lambda-to-the-k times S-inverse. Lambda-to-the-k just raises each diagonal entry: lambda-one-to-the-k, lambda-two-to-the-k. Two numbers. That's it.
But watch — a matrix appears with a repeated eigenvalue and only one independent eigenvector. S can't be built. The factorization breaks. Not every matrix diagonalizes.
When it works, diagonalization turns thousand-step powers into a single clean formula.
